coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots-based surface protein-imprinted nanoparticles via sandwich-structured template pre-assemble and post-imprinting modification for enhanced fluorescence detection
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Siyu
Li, Dongru
Feng, Xingjia
Fu, Guoqi
coredata.dc:description: Molecularly imprinted polymers combined with fluorescence sensing show potential application in biological analysis and diseases diagnosis. We illustrate a versatile strategy for synthesis of surface protein-imprinted nanoparticles via sandwich-structured template pre-assemble and post-imprinting modification (PIM) for enhanced fluorescence detection. Carbon dots anchored SiO2 nanoparticles are surface modified with both disulfanylpropanoic acid and vinyl groups. By amide linkage to the carboxyl terminals, protein templates are immobilized to the nanoparticles, and subsequently allowed to bind with functional monomers via strong interactions such as metal coordination and enzyme–inhibitor association. The sandwiched protein templates are surface imprinted by free radical copolymerization with other monomers. The templates are then removed from the imprinted shells by reduction of the disulfide bonds. The derived thiol groups are used for PIM of the imprinted cavities with carboxyl groups capable of electrostatic interaction with the protein rebound. For proof of concept, human serum albumin (HSA) was firstly used as a model template for surface imprinting. It was shown that binding of a Cu2+-chelating monomer to the immobilized HSA and the subsequent PIM could elevate the imprinting factor from 1.6 to 4.1 and further to 7.6, respectively. The final imprinted nanoparticles exhibited a low limit of detection of 9.8 nM and satisfactory recoveries ranging from 98.1 to 102.0% for measuring HSA in diluted human serum samples. This strategy was further confirmed by using trypsin, a typical serine protease, as another protein template, with both the Cu2+-chelating monomer and an enzyme inhibitor monomer binding to the surface coupled template.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Protein imprinting
Metal coordination
Enzyme–inhibitor interaction
Carbon dots
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface functionalization of FL SiO2 NPs with vinyl and disulfanylpropanoic acid
Post-imprinting modification of FL HSA-imprinted NPs
Synthesis and post-imprinting modification of FL trypsin-imprinted NPs
Synthesis and characterization of CDs-based FL HSA-imprinted NPs
FL properties of FL HSA-imprinted NPs
Optimized synthesis and post-imprinting modification of FL HSA-imprinted NPs
FL sensing performance of the MIP-PIM NPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21674051 ) and the National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFC1808603).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) FL SiO2-MPS/SS-COOH, (b) MIP and (c) NIP NPs. The insets are the high-resolution pictures with a bar of 50 nm.

(A) Zeta potentials of FL SiO2 NPs (a), FL SiO2-MPS/IPTES-AEPD NPs (b), FL SiO2-MPS/SS-COOH NPs (c), FL SiO2-MPS/HSA NPs (d), FL SiO2-MPS/HSA-(GMA-IDA-Cu2+) (e), NIP NPs (f), MIP NPs (g), MIP-PIM NPs before loading of Cu2+ (h), and MIP-PIM NPs (i). (B) TG curves of FL SiO2-MPS/SS-COOH NPs after reduction with TCEP (a), MIP NPs (b), and NIP NPs (c).
Absorption spectrum of pyridine-2-thione and the calibration curve for its quantitation at λ = 359 nm in methanol.

(A) The absorption spectrum of HSA (30 μM) (a), and FL emission spectra of the MIP NPs in the absence of HSA (b) and in the presence of HSA(0.4 μM) (c), and the FL SiO2 NPs (d). The insets presents the images of the MIP dispersions under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right), respectively. (B) The FL emission intensity change of the MIP and NIP NPs in phosphate buffer during continuous FL measurements. (C) FL response kinetics of the MIP and NIP NPs to HSA (0.4 μM).

(A) The FL response of the HSA-imprinted NPs and non-imprinted NPs to HSA fabricated differently. (B) Comparison of the FL response of the HSA-imprinted NPs to HSA with that to BSA. (a) Synthesized without use of GMA-IDA-Cu2+, (b) synthesized using GMA-IDA-Cu2+, and (c) synthesized using GMA-IDA-Cu2+ followed by PIM with carboxylic groups. The bottom graphs illustrate the structures of the corresponding imprinted cavities.

The FL emission spectra of (a) the MIP-PIM and (b) NIP-PIM NPs in the presence of HSA with increased concentrations (0–0.4 μM), respectively. The insets show the corresponding Stern–Volmer plots with the estimated K SV and R 2. (c) FL enhancement of the MIP-PIM and NIP-PIM NPs in the presence of different proteins (0.4 μM). (d) FL response of the MIP-PIM NPs to HSA (0.4 μM) mixed with BSA of multifold higher concentrations.

(A) The FL response of the trypsin-imprinted NPs and non-imprinted NPs to trypsin constructed differently. (B) Comparison of the FL response of the trypsin-imprinted NPs to trypsin with that to Lyz. (a) Synthesized without use of either GMA-IDA-Cu2+ or AB, (b) synthesized using GMA-IDA-Cu2+, and (c) synthesized using both GMA-IDA-Cu2+ and AB, (d) synthesized using both GMA-IDA-Cu2+ and AB followed by the PIM with carboxylic groups. The bottom graphs illustrate the structures of the corresponding imprinted cavities, respectively.

The FL emission spectra of (a) the trypsin-MIP-PIM and (b) NIP-PIM NPs in the presence of trypsin with increased concentrations (0–0.8 μM), respectively. The insets show the corresponding Stern–Volmer plots with the estimated K SV and R 2. (c) FL enhancement of the trypsin-MIP-PIM and NIP-PIM NPs in the presence of different proteins (0.8 μM). (d) FL response of the MIP-PIM NPs to trypsin (0.8 μM) mixed with Lyz of multifold higher concentrations.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of FL HSA-imprinted NPs over CDs-anchored SiO2 NPs via sandwich-structured template pre-assemble and disulfide exchange based post-imprinting modification.
Comparison of the analytical performances with the reported MI-FL sensors for BSA or HSA detection.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots-based surface protein-imprinted nanoparticles via sandwich-structured template pre-assemble and post-imprinting modification for enhanced fluorescence detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer Materials of Ministry of Education, Institute of Polymer Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer Materials of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer Materials of Ministry of Education, Institute of Polymer Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecularly imprinted polymers combined with fluorescence sensing show potential application in biological analysis and diseases diagnosis. We illustrate a versatile strategy for synthesis of surface protein-imprinted nanoparticles via sandwich-structured template pre-assemble and post-imprinting modification (PIM) for enhanced fluorescence detection. Carbon dots anchored SiO2 nanoparticles are surface modified with both disulfanylpropanoic acid and vinyl groups. By amide linkage to the carboxyl terminals, protein templates are immobilized to the nanoparticles, and subsequently allowed to bind with functional monomers via strong interactions such as metal coordination and enzyme–inhibitor association. The sandwiched protein templates are surface imprinted by free radical copolymerization with other monomers. The templates are then removed from the imprinted shells by reduction of the disulfide bonds. The derived thiol groups are used for PIM of the imprinted cavities with carboxyl groups capable of electrostatic interaction with the protein rebound. For proof of concept, human serum albumin (HSA) was firstly used as a model template for surface imprinting. It was shown that binding of a Cu2+-chelating monomer to the immobilized HSA and the subsequent PIM could elevate the imprinting factor from 1.6 to 4.1 and further to 7.6, respectively. The final imprinted nanoparticles exhibited a low limit of detection of 9.8 nM and satisfactory recoveries ranging from 98.1 to 102.0% for measuring HSA in diluted human serum samples. This strategy was further confirmed by using trypsin, a typical serine protease, as another protein template, with both the Cu2+-chelating monomer and an enzyme inhibitor monomer binding to the surface coupled template.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imprinting is a vital methodology for artificial synthesis of affinity materials with antibody-like binding sites. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are synthesized by copolymerization of functional and crosslinking monomers in the presence of a template. After template removal, tailor-made imprinted cavities complementary to the template in shape, size and functionality are generated, which can specifically recognize the template molecules. MIPs have been widely used in a variety of fields, such as affinity separation media, molecular recognition elements for sensors, and specific target-capturing materials in ligand binding assays [1]. The imprinting of proteins and other biomacromolecules is challenged by their large size, complex structure and variable conformation [2–4]. As a result, surface imprinting over different nanomaterials, e.g. SiO2 or Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs), carbon nanotubes and quantum dots (QDs), has received much attention due to the advantages such as ease of macromolecular diffusion, large surface area, and facile utilization of the functions of the coated nanomaterials [5]. Moreover, much lower crosslinking degrees are usually employed than for imprinting of small molecules.

Recently, sensitive detection of proteins and other large biological entities by combining the selective recognition features of the MIPs with the sensing properties of fluorescence (FL) substances has attracted large number of studies [4,6,7–9]. The FL substances used for construction of the molecular imprinting-based FL sensors (MI-FL sensors) include inorganic semiconductor QDs (e.g. CdTe, CdS and Mn-ZnS QDs) [10–17], carbon-based carbon quantum dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [18–23] and lanthanide-doped upconversion NPs (UCNPs) [24]. Among them, CDs show superiority in terms of facile synthesis as well as modification, robust chemical inertness, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [25].

The sensing performance of a MI-FL sensor is usually characterized by the limit of detection (LOD), imprinting factor based on the FL response ratio of the MIPs with respect to the corresponding non-imprinted polymers (NIPs), and spike recovery from real samples [6]. For strengthening the sensing performance, one kind of methods is construction of ratiometric FL detection system using two FL substances, with one being the target-sensitive and another as an inactive reference [17,23]. However, such double-emission FL sensors are much more difficult to fabricate than single-emission sensors. On the other hand, any measures beneficial to enhancing the specific recognition of MIPs can be adopted to improve their optosensing properties. In this context, a variety of FL glycoprotein-imprinted sensors were fabricated by use of vinylized or surface immobilized phenylboronic acids, which can form reversible covalent linkage of the cis-diol-containing glycoproteins [14,20,26,27]. Our previous study showed that post-imprinting PEGylation of the CdTe QDs-based protein-imprinted NPs could improve the FL sensing by reducing non-specific binding [28]. Recently, we reported surface protein imprinting over CDs-anchored SiO2 NPs for enhanced FL sensing by copolymerization of a Cu2+-chelating monomer and an oligo(ethylene glycol) (OEG) monomer, since that the metal coordination can significantly strengthen protein imprinting and the resulting non-linear PEG moieties can significantly reduce non-specific binding [29]. Tan et al. [30] synthesized highly efficient QDs-based protein-imprinted NPs for FL detection using thiol-terminated aptamers as a functional monomer capable of binding to the protein in a specific manner analogous to antibody–antigen interactions; however, it is not an easy task for screening and synthesizing of aptamers toward a specific template.

For achieving sensitive FL response, Takeuchi and co-workers [31,32] pioneered post-imprinting modification (PIM) of the imprinted cavities with FL reporters or/and the functional groups which can interact with the re-bound templates. This approach involved fabrication of an imprinted film over a flat gold-coated substrate, modification of protein templates and synthesis of a series of organic molecules, hence being difficult to follow. This PIM concept may be adapted for construction of protein-imprinted NPs.

Herein, we demonstrate a versatile strategy for synthesis of surface protein-imprinted nanoparticles for enhancing FL detection via sandwich-structured template pre-assemble and PIM. Through amide linkage, protein templates were coupled to the surface of CDs-anchored SiO2 NPs modified with both vinyl and disulfanylpropanoic acid groups. The immobilized protein molecules were allowed to strongly interact with the monomers by metal coordination or enzyme–inhibitor association. The as-sandwiched protein templates were surface imprinted via aqueous copolymerization with OEG-based monomer and other monomers. Template removal from the imprinted shells was then carried out by reduction of the disulfide bonds. The resultant thiols within the imprinted cavities were used to introduce carboxylic groups capable of electrostatic interacting with the target protein while rebinding. Human serum albumin (HSA) and trypsin with exposed histidine (His) residues were chosen as model protein templates to prove the efficacy of the proposed strategy for enhancing FL detection, respectively.

Tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS), 3-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (MPS), 3-mercaptopropyltrimethoxysilane (MPTMS), tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), iminodiacetic acid (IDA), 3-mercaptopropanoic acid (MPA), bicinchoninic acid disodium salt (BCA), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 2,2′-dipyridyl disulfide were purchased from Aladdin, China. (3-Isocyanatopropyl)triethoxysilane (IPTES) was purchased from Heowns Biochem, China. Glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), ammonium persulfate (APS, recrystallized before use), acrylic acid (AA) and ethylenediamine were obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagents Co., China. Di(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate (MEO2MA) was purchased from TCI, China. Acrylamide (AAm, electrophoresis grade), N,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, electrophoresis grade), dodecyl sodium sulfate (SDS), cysteine hydrochloride, N,N,N’,N’-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED) and cytochrome c (Cyt c) were purchased from Sangon, China. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme (Lyz), human serum albumin (HSA), ovalbumin (OVA), trypsin and oligo(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate with a Mn of about 500 (OEGMA-500) were obtained from Sigma, China. S-(2-Pyridylthio)cysteamine hydrochloride (AEPD·HCl) synthesized according to the reference method [33]. (2-pyridyldithio)propionic acid (PDPA) was synthesized according to the reference [34]. 4-Acrylamidophenyl (amino)methaniminium chloride (AB) was synthesized according to the reference [35]. All the other chemicals were used as received unless otherwise stated. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (>18.6 MΩcm) produced by a UPR-II purification system (Chengdu YouPu Biotech Co., China).

FL SiO2 NPs were synthesized according to our recent work [29]. Briefly, SiO2 NPs were synthesized by the Stöber method [36] and then modified with MPTMS to obtain thiol-functionalized SiO2 NPs (denoted as SiO2-SH). GMA functionalized CDs (denoted as GMA-CDs) were synthesized by the reaction of GMA with the amine groups on the CDs’ surface. The GMA-CDs were then anchored to the surface of the SiO2-SH NPs via thiol-Michael addition click reaction catalyzed by TCEP, followed by coating with a thin SiO2 layer by modified Stöber method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface functionalization of FL SiO2 NPs with vinyl and disulfanylpropanoic acid
ce:section.ce:para: AEPD·HCl (133 mg, 600 μmol), IPTES (0.15 mL, 600 μmol) and triethylamine (0.253 mL, 180 μmol) were mixed in dry tetrahydrofuran (4 mL), and the mixture was stirred in argon atmosphere at 25 °C for 24 h. After centrifugation, the supernatant was evaporated to a yellow oil, a silane coupling agent denoted as IPTES-AEPD [37]. The as-synthesized IPTES-AEPD and MPS (66 μL) were added to the dried FL SiO2 NPs (0.5 g) dispersed in dry methylbenzene (10 mL), and the mixture was stirred in argon atmosphere at 70 °C for 12 h. After washing with ethanol, the resultant functionalized NPs (denoted as FL SiO2-MPS/ IPTES-AEPD) were re-dispersed in 10 mL of methanol containing MPA (22 μL, 0.25 mmol), and the mixture was stirred in argon atmosphere at 25 °C for 12 h. The as-functionalized NPs, denoted as FL SiO2-MPS/SS-COOH, were washed with ethanol and then dried under vacuum. The grafting amount of carboxylic groups was estimated by monitoring the absorption of the released pyridin-2-thione at 359 nm with a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The Cu2+-chelating monomer (denoted as GMA-IDA-Cu2+) was synthesized according to the literature method [38], by stirring the mixture consisting of GMA (12.5 mmol), IDA (12.5 mmol) and NaOH (25 mmol) in 50 mL of distillated water at 50 °C for 2 h, adjusting the solution to neutrality with concentrated hydrochloric acid, mixing with 12.5 mmol of CuSO4 (0.25 M), and finally re-adjusting to neutrality.

For the synthesis of FL HSA-imprinted NPs, HSA was firstly coupled to the FL SiO2-MPS/SS-COOH NPs. The NPs (75 mg), EDC·HCl (1.8 mg, 9.9 μmol) and NHS (2.3 mg, 13.2 μmol) were dissolved in dry DMF (1.5 mL), and the dispersion was stirred at 25 °C for 4 h. After washing with DMF, the carboxyl-activated NPs were re-dispersed in DMF (0.75 mL) and then mixed with 6.75 mL of PBS buffer (0.1 M sodium phosphate + 0.15 M NaCl, pH 7.0) containing HSA (15 mg), and the mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 4 h. The excess activated carboxyl groups on the surface of the NPs were blocked with Tris buffer (100 mM, pH 8.0) for 30 min, resulting in FL SiO2-MPS/HSA NPs. These HSA-attached NPs were further incubated in 3 mL of 8 times diluted GMA-IDA-Cu2+ solution obtained above at 25 °C for 20 min, affording FL SiO2-MPS/HSA-(GMA-IDA-Cu2+) NPs.

Surface imprinting over the FL SiO2-MPS/HSA-(GMA-IDA-Cu2+) NPs was conducted as follows. These NPs were dispersed in 7.5 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 10 mM) containing MEO2MA (20.75 mg, 110.67 μmol), OEGMA-500 (9.72 mg, 19.53 μmol), MBA (5.71 mg, 37.2 μmol) and AAm (1.32 mg, 18.6 μmol) with a total monomer concentration of 0.5% (w/v) and a molar crosslinking degree of 20%. After purging with nitrogen for 15 min, 0.15 mL of APS solution (3.75%, w/v) and 0.15 mL of TEMED (2.42%, v/v) solution were added to the suspension subsequently, and polymerization was continued under magnetic stirring at 30 °C for 15 h. Afterwards, removal of HSA from the surface-imprinted NPs was carried out by subsequent washing with EDTA-2Na+ solution (200 mM) for 30 min to desorb Cu2+ and with TCEP solution (10 mM) for 2 h to reduce the disulfides, followed by repeated washing with 1% SDS/1% acetic acid solution to remove the embedded HSA until no protein in the supernatant was detected using a UV/Vis spectrophotometer at 280 nm. NaCl solution (1 M) was used to remove the remaining SDS and acetic acid. The NPs were then incubated in CuSO4 solution (0.1 M) for 30 min to reload Cu2+, washed with NaCl solution (1 M) and water, and finally freeze-dried for further use. The control non-imprinted particles were prepared in the same way but polymerization over the FL SiO2-MPS/SS-COOH NPs without coupling of HSA. The final imprinted and non-imprinted particles are named as MIP and NIP NPs, respectively.

The surface imprinting over the FL SiO2-MPS/HSA NPs without binding with GMA-IDA-Cu2+ was also carried out by the same way. The resulting imprinted and non-imprinted particles were denoted as MIP-control and NIP-control, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Post-imprinting modification of FL HSA-imprinted NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The MIP or NIP NPs (25 mg) without loading of Cu2+ were incubated in 2.5 mL of methanol containing PDPA (0.5 mg, 2.3 μmol) under argon at 25 °C for 12 h, extensively washed with methanol, and then loaded with Cu2+ as described above. The resulting products were denoted as MIP-PIM and NIP-PIM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and post-imprinting modification of FL trypsin-imprinted NPs
ce:section.ce:para: See supporting information.

FL spectra were recorded using a fluorospectrophotometer (F-2710, Hitachi, Japan), with the silt widths of excitation and emission set at 5 nm and 10 nm, respectively, and the photomultiplier tube voltage set at 700 V. A λ ex of 320 nm was chosen according to our previous study [29]. Unless stated otherwise, all the NPs for FL measurements were dispersed in phosphate buffer (PB, pH 7.0, 10 mM) with a concentration of 200 μg/mL. UV–Vis absorption was measured on a spectrophotometer (TU-1900, Purkinje General Instrument Co., China) equipped with a temperature controller. The morphologies and structures of the NPs were observed by a TEM (Talos F200X G2, FEI, Czech) and a SEM (Apreo S LoVac, FEI, Czech). Zeta potentials of the NPs were measured by DLS (Zeta Sizer Nano-ZS90, Malvern, UK). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TG 209, NETZSCH, Germany) under nitrogen atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min up to 850 °C. FT-IR spectra were determined over KBr pellets on a FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, ThermoFisher Scientific, USA).

Human serum was employed to examine the practicability and accuracy of the MIP-PIM NPs for target protein detection. For HSA detection, the human serum was diluted 4000-fold with phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 10 mM) to ensure the HSA concentration within the linear detection range. The MIP-PIM NPs (200 μg) were added into the diluted human serum solution (1 mL) spiked with the target protein at three concentration levels. The FL spectra of the dispersion were recorded and all the tests were performed in triplicates. Trypsin was detected by the same way except for use of trypsin-imprinted NPs and undiluted human serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs-based FL HSA-imprinted NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of FL HSA-imprinted NPs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The CDs-attached FL SiO2 NPs were synthesized according to our previous method [29]. Surface vinyl and disulfanylpropanoic acid groups were introduced by one-pot silylation of the FL SiO2 NPs with two silane coupling agents and subsequent thiol-disulfide exchange between the terminal dipyridyl disulfide with MPA. After activation of the carboxylic groups with EDC/NHS, HSA molecules were coupled to the FL SiO2 NPs via formation of amide linkages between the surface carboxylic groups and the amine groups on HSA. The immobilized HSA with two exposed His residues were further allowed to bind with a Cu2+-chelating monomer, GMA-IDA-Cu2+, through relatively strong metal coordination, hence affording sandwich-structured template pre-assemble. Surface protein imprinting over the functionalized FL SiO2 NPs was conducted by aqueous polymerization, with AAm as the functional monomer for hydrogen bonding with the protein, OEG monomers (MEO2MA and OEGMA-500) as principal monomers capable of forming nonlinear PEG moieties, and MBA as a cross-linker. Template removal from the imprinted NPs was then performed after elution of the Cu2+ with EDTA and reduction of the disulfide bonds with TCEP, leaving behind thiol groups. Finally, carboxylic groups were introduced through the thiols by disulfide exchange, and Cu2+ was reloaded to the IDA moieties in the imprinted coatings. When the resultant HSA-imprinted NPs are employed for rebinding of HSA, the as-introduced carboxylic groups can form strong electrostatic interactions with the amine groups on HSA, through which the protein molecules are originally immobilized. Meanwhile, the metal coordination between the IDA-Cu2+ and HSA can be restored to the state before template removal. Both such electrostatic interactions and metal coordination are expected to enhance FL response while rebinding of HSA to the imprinted cavities.

As seen from the TEM image in Fig. 1 a, the size of the CD-anchored FL SiO2 NPs was about 150 nm. The successful surface functionalization was proved by a series of zeta potential measurements. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the NPs became decreased negatively charged after modification with the two neutral silanes (IPTES-AEPD and MPS), while showed increased negative charge after subsequent introduction of carboxylic groups by the disulfide exchange. Significant charge variation was also observed after further coupling of HSA and binding of GMA-IDA-Cu2+ to the immobilized protein. The surface functionalization of the FL SiO2 NPs was further verified by FT-IR analysis (see Fig. S1) and some quantitative analyses. The amount of the carboxylic groups can be calculated according to the equimolar pyridine-2-thione released from the disulfide exchange reaction, which was quantified by monitoring the increase in absorbance at 359 nm in methanol with the calibration curve shown in Fig. 3 . It was estimated to be 44.1 μmol per gram of the NPs. The amount of immobilized HSA was determined to be 11.1 mg (∼0.17 μmol) per gram of the NPs with the BCA method [39] to assay the protein concentrations before and after the coupling reaction. This means that only about 0.4% of the surface carboxylic groups were conjugated with HSA molecules, owing to the large protein size and surface steric hindrance.

The resultant core–shell MIP and NIP NPs were observed by TEM, respectively. As shown from the TEM images in Fig. 1b and c, these NPs remained the spherical morphologies like the original FL SiO2 NPs; however, the polymer shells could not be discriminated probably due to their small thickness in the dried state. This is consistent with the result observed from the core–shell imprinted NPs reported previously [29]. The formation of the MIP or NIP shells was further verified by zeta potential measurements, TGA and the following FL analyses. As shown in Fig. 2A, the MIP and NIP NPs showed remarkably elevated charges in comparison with that of any of the precursor NPs, indicating the existing of the polymer layers. As can be seen from Fig. 2B, TGA substantially confirmed the formation of the MIP and NIP polymer shells, which were estimated to be 11.2 wt% and 8.6 wt%, respectively, relative to the inside nanocores.

As shown in Fig. 2A, successful introduction of carboxylic groups to the imprinted cavities by reaction of the thiols with PDPA was proved by the noticeably lowered zeta potential of the resulting MIP-PIM NPs. Moreover, this was also verified by the equimolar pyridine-2-thione released from the reaction. According to the absorbance of the pyridine-2-thione at 359 nm, the amount of carboxylic groups was measured to be 11.7 μmol per gram of the MIP-PIM NPs. This value was much lower than the amount of the carboxylic groups initially introduced on the FL SiO2 NPs (44.1 μmol/g, as given above), probably since that the MIP shells could hinder the PDPA molecules to reach the thiols situated at the core–shell interface. Considering the relatively small amount of HSA immobilized on the FL SiO2 NPs (0.17 μmol/g) via amide linkage before imprinting, however, such amount of carboxylic groups introduced via PIM was sufficient for enhancement of HSA rebinding to the imprinted cavities by electrostatic interaction with their amine groups of HSA, through which they were originally attached to the FL SiO2 NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL properties of FL HSA-imprinted NPs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 A, the FL SiO2 NPs and the MIP NPs shared emission peaks at λ em of 394 nm. But the FL intensity of the core–shell imprinted NPs was greatly lower than that of the FL SiO2 NPs. This can be explained as follows. The FL emission originated from the carbon dots within the FL SiO2 NPs; however, the MIP shells formed over the FL SiO2 NPs were not only FL inert, but also could weaken the FL emitted from the FL SiO2 NPs. It is worthwhile to note that, the MIP NPs exhibited FL enhancement in the presence of HSA (so-called “turn-on” type), which is beneficial for sensitive FL detection in comparison with the corresponding “turn-off” type showing FL quenching [6]. On the other hand, the FL emission spectra showed no spectral overlap with the absorption spectrum of HSA, which suggested that the FL enhancement may be ascribed to the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) between the CDs and the protein [6]. These results are consistent to these observed in our previous study with surface imprinting of Lyz over the similar CDs-anchored SiO2 NPs [29]. As shown in Fig. 4B, the FL emission of the MIP and NIP NPs remained stable within 20 min, long enough for FL measurements. On incubation of the MIP as well as the NIP NPs with HSA, FL intensities increased to the constant levels within about 10 min (see Fig. 4C). This FL response speed is compared to that of the QDs-based surface protein-imprinted nanomaterials [15,28] and the CDs-based Lyz-imprinted NPs [29].

The FL enhancement is defined as F/F0 − 1, where F0 and F are the FL intensities in the absence and presence of an analyst, respectively. The ratio of F/F0 − 1 for MIP relative to that for the NIP, denoted as (F/F0 − 1)MIP/NIP, can be used to estimate the FL response attributed to the specific binding to the imprinted cavities. As shown in Fig. S2, the FL response of the MIP and NIP NPs to HSA at different temperatures and pH values was investigated, respectively. The highest levels of (F/F0 − 1)MIP/NIP were observed at 25 °C and pH 7.0, respectively, and therefore these conditions were chosen in the following study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimized synthesis and post-imprinting modification of FL HSA-imprinted NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The thickness of the core–shell MIP NPs may play an important role on their FL response, especially for surface imprinting with immobilized templates. The polymerization for the surface imprinting was initiated by a redox couple, TEMED/APS, with TEMED as an accelerating agent. More TEMED added may facilitate the polymerization in the solution, while not advantageous to surface imprinting polymerization over the HSA-attached FL SiO2 NPs. Therefore, different mass ratios of TEMED/APS (from 25% to 100%) were tested in order to optimize the FL response of the resultant MIP NPs. As shown in Fig. S3, the highest (F/F0 − 1)MIP/NIP was acquired with TEMED/APS being 50%. The FL intensities of MIP NPs decreased with reducing the mass ratios of TEMED/APS, indicative of the gradual increase of the imprinted layer thickness as expected.

Both the binding of Cu2+-chelating monomer (GMA-IDA-Cu2+) to the immobilized HSA and PIM of the imprinted cavities with carboxylic groups could remarkably enhance the FL response of the resultant imprinted NPs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the imprinted NPs synthesized with neither GMA-IDA-Cu2+ nor PIM exhibited a (F/F0 − 1)MIP/NIP of 1.6, while binding of GMA-IDA-Cu2+ to the HSA made it increase to 4.1, and the further PIM step elevated it up to 7.6. With BSA, a homologous protein of HSA with the similar amino acid sequence, Mw and pI, as a reference protein, the FL response selectivity of the imprinted NPs can be expressed as the ratio of (F/F0 − 1)HSA/ (F/F0 − 1)BSA. As can be seen from Fig. 5B, the Cu2+-chelating monomer binding and PIM step could also noticeably enhance (F/F0 − 1)HSA/ (F/F0 − 1)BSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL sensing performance of the MIP-PIM NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
For confirming the ability of the HSA-imprinted NPs for target protein detection, the FL spectra of the MIP-PIM and NIP-PIM NPs were recorded in HSA solutions with C HSA ranging from 0 to 0.4 μM. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, both the MIP-PIM and NIP-PIM NPs illustrated FL enhancement in the presence of HSA. However, the increase degrees for the MIP-PIM NPs were significantly larger than that for the NIP-PIM controls due to the recognition cavities constructed in the imprinted shells. The FL enhancement defined as F/F0 − 1, follows the Stern − Volmer equation.
(1)
F /
F 0
- 1 =
K
sv
C
where K SV is the Stern − Volmer constant, and C is the analyte concentration [11,15]. The ratio of K SV,MIP / K SV,NIP is defined as imprinting factor (IFSV). Within the studied concentration range, F/F0 − 1 for both MIP-PIM and NIP-PIM NPs could be well fitted to the Stern − Volmer equation. The K sv values were estimated to be 1.186 and 0.160 μM−1 for the imprinted and non-imprinted NPs, respectively, hence with the IFSV computed to be about 7.4. The limit of detection (LOD) of the imprinted NPs was figured out to be 9.8 nM, following the criteria (3σ/s), where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurements, and s the slope of calibration curve (herein, s = K sv) [10–24]. The HSA-imprinted NPs produced without PIM, or with neither GMA-IDA-Cu2+ nor PIM, showed significantly lower performance in terms of both IFSV and LOD, as listed in Table S1, which were determined in the same way. As can be seen from Table 1 , the MIP-PIM NPs also showed outstanding detection performance in comparison with the other MI-FL sensors for HSA or BSA reported previously.

The selectivity of the MIP-PIM NPs for HSA was confirmed by comparison of their FL response to HSA with that to reference proteins having different Mw, isoelectric point (pI) and number of exposed His residues at a same concentration (0.4 μM). All the proteins studied were HSA (Mw 66 k, pI 4.8, 2 His), bovine serum albumin (BSA, Mw 67 k, pI 4.8, 2 His), ovalbumin (OVA, Mw 44.5 k, pI 4.5, 1 His), Lyz (Mw 14 k, pI 11.1, 1 His), cytochrome c (Cyt c, Mw 12.4 k, pI 10.2, 1 His) and trypsin (Mw 24 k, pI 10.5, 1 His) [45]. It is worthwhile to note that HSA and BSA are homologous proteins sharing approximately 80% amino acid identity [46]. In this sense, choosing BSA as a reference protein can severely test the recognition selectivity of the HSA-imprinted NPs. As shown in Fig. 6c, the MIP-PIM NPs showed over 10 times higher F/F0-1 toward HSA than toward BSA and other reference proteins, and also exhibited remarkably higher F/F0-1 level than that of the non-imprinted NPs toward all the proteins investigated. Furthermore, the F/F0-1 level of the MIP-PIM NPs toward HSA (0.4 μM) was almost not affected by mixing with the homologous protein, BSA, as a competitive protein with up to five-fold higher concentration (Fig. 6d). These results confirmed that high-quality HSA-imprinted cavities were generated within the MIP-PIM NPs.

The potential application of the MIP-PIM NPs for FL detection of HSA in real bio-samples was tested in diluted human serum solutions spiked with different HSA concentrations. The human serum was 4000-fold diluted in order to make the HSA concentration within the linear range of the above obtained calibration curve. As shown in Table S2, satisfactory recoveries and accuracies were attained with a recovery range of 98.1–102.0% and RSDs of 4.2–9.4% for the spiked samples with three HSA concentrations. Therefore, the MIP-PIM NPs may be employed for FL detection of low HSA concentrations in real samples.

The MIP-PIM NPs after used for FL measurements in the presence of HSA were regenerated by template removal in the same way as the imprinted particles were initially synthesized, re-loaded with Cu2+, and then reused for FL analysis. Six cycles of use/regeneration were examined. As shown in Fig. S4, the FL response of the MIP-PIM NPs to HSA remained nearly stable after 4 cycles of use/regeneration, and then began to decrease slowly. This suggested that the imprinted cavities would become impaired after repeated treatment with the template removal agent consisting of SDS and acetic acid.

We further synthesized trypsin-imprinted NPs for two purposes. One is to examine the generality of the proposed approach by sandwich-structured template pre-assemble based on the metal coordination with a protein carrying exposed His residues, because trypsin has one exposed His residue. Another purpose is to prove that, besides the metal coordination, other kinds of interactions such as enzyme–inhibitor binding can also be exploited for construction of the sandwich structure, since trypsin is a typical serine protease belonging to a large protein family, and benzamidine is a well-known inhibitor of a number of serine proteases [47]. Therefore, the strong metal coordination and enzyme–inhibitor interaction can be synergistically utilized to confer sandwich-like template pre-assemble. As illustrated in Scheme S1, the procedure for the surface trypsin imprinting and subsequent PIM over the functionalized FL SiO2 NPs is same as the case of above HSA imprinting, except that, following the metal-chelating monomer (GMA-IDA-Cu2+), benzamidine-derived functional monomer (AB) was further attached to the immobilized template protein.

The FL response of the resulting trypsin-imprinted NPs was measured in the presence of trypsin (0.8 μM) at λ ex = 320 nm with enhancement emission peaks at λ em = 394 nm. Compared with direct imprinting over the trypsin-coupled FL SiO2 NPs, As shown in Fig. 7 A, sequentially anchoring of GMA-IDA-Cu2+ and AB to the immobilized template, and further PIM of the imprinted NPs with carboxylic groups caused the (F/F0-1)MIP/NIP to increase from 3.9 to 5.1, to 6.1, and finally to 13.1, respectively. These steps could also greatly enhance the FL response selectivity against Lyz, a reference protein with approached Mw and pI (see Fig. 7B). As shown in Fig. 8 , the final MIP-PIM NPs afforded a IFsv of 12.9 and a LOD of 7.1 nM with a linear detection range of 0.1–0.8 μM, and showed good FL response selectivity. This level of LOD was slightly lower than achieved with the trypsin-imprinted nanogels, which were fabricated by a solid synthesis approach [48]. Their LOD was converted to be about 15 nM based on the reported limit of quantification, which is usually defined as 10σ/s [49], different from the LOD (3σ/s) as defined above. As can be seen from Table S3, the MIP-PIM NPs also exhibited satisfactory recoveries (98.5–101.5%) and accuracies (0.5–1.5%) for FL detection of trypsin in spiked human serum samples.

In summary, with both HSA and trypsin as model protein templates bearing exposed His residues, we have demonstrated a strategy for synthesis of FL surface protein-imprinted NPs for enhanced FL detection via sandwich-structured template pre-assemble and post-imprinting modification. Protein templates were coupled to both disulfanylpropanoic acid and vinyl functionalized FL SiO2 NPs via amide linkage, and then allowed to bind with special monomers via strong interactions like metal coordination or enzyme–inhibitor association, followed by surface imprinting. The derived thiol groups within the imprinted cavities were modified with carboxyl groups for electrostatic interaction with the targets re-bound. The as-synthesized protein-imprinted NPs exhibited remarkable FL sensing performance in terms to IFSV, LOD, selectivity, and recoveries from spiked human serum samples.

This can be a versatile approach for the construction of high-performance FL protein-imprinted NPs, since that most proteins bear exposed His residues so as to be pre-assembled by use of metal coordination. Moreover, it may be further exploited by utilization of other kind of strong interactions with proteins, e.g. formation of borate ester between cis-diol-containing glycoproteins with phenylboric acid.

Siyu Jin: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Dongru Li: Investigation. Xingjia Feng: Investigation. Guoqi Fu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declares that there are no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that may affect the work reported in this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21674051) and the National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFC1808603).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107611.

ce:section.ce:para:

Bioelectron. 48 (2013) 113–119.

Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Hedstrom, Serine protease mechanism and specificity, Chem. Rev. 2002 (102) 4501–4523.

Sens. Actuators, B

Anal. Chim. Acta
